questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with fever, headache, and myalgia 10 days after returning from a safari in South Africa. She has a 2 cm eschar on her left thigh and tender left inguinal lymphadenopathy. Temperature 39.2°C. Blood tests: platelets 120 × 10⁹/L, normal LFTs. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The eschar and regional lymphadenopathy are classic for rickettsial infection, specifically African tick typhus (Rickettsia africae). Falciparum malaria is possible in South Africa, but does not cause an eschar. Dengue typically presents with rash (not eschar) and severe myalgia. Leptospirosis often has conjunctival suffusion, not an eschar. Meningococcaemia has a petechial rash, not a single eschar.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with fever, severe headache, and arthralgia 5 days after returning from a holiday in Thailand. He has a diffuse maculopapular rash. Temperature 39.5°C. Blood tests: WBC 3.2 × 10⁹/L (neutropenia), platelets 90 × 10⁹/L, normal LFTs. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Dengue fever typically presents with fever, severe headache, arthralgia, rash, and leukopenia/thrombocytopenia. The rapid onset after return from Thailand is typical. Falciparum malaria causes thrombocytopenia but not leukopenia and has periodic fevers; chikungunya has more severe joint pain; typhoid has relative bradycardia and rose spots; Zika has milder symptoms and conjunctivitis.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old student presents with fever, abdominal pain, and loose stools 3 weeks after returning from a gap year in India. Temperature 39.8°C, pulse 90 bpm (relative bradycardia). He has a faint pink macular rash on his trunk. Blood tests: WBC 5.5 × 10⁹/L, platelets 200 × 10⁹/L, LFTs elevated (ALT 120 U/L). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Relative bradycardia (pulse 90 with temp 39.8°C) and rose spots are classic for typhoid fever (Salmonella typhi). Abdominal pain and loose stools also typical. Dengue does not cause relative bradycardia; leptospirosis often has conjunctival suffusion; malaria has periodic fevers; viral hepatitis has more prominent jaundice and pruritus.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman presents with fever, confusion, and jaundice 2 weeks after returning from a trip to rural Ghana. She took no malaria prophylaxis. Temperature 40.2°C, GCS 14/15, pulse 110 bpm, BP 90/60 mmHg. Blood film shows high parasitaemia with ring forms. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: High parasitaemia with ring forms confirms severe falciparum malaria. The patient has cerebral malaria (confusion) and jaundice, with no prophylaxis. Dengue haemorrhagic fever has thrombocytopenia and plasma leakage, not high parasitaemia; typhoid encephalopathy is rare and without parasitaemia; yellow fever has jaundice but no ring forms; meningococcal meningitis has neck stiffness and CSF findings.

questions.stem: A 31-year-old man presents with fever, dry cough, and urticaria 5 weeks after swimming in Lake Malawi. He has no diarrhoea. Temperature 38.5°C. Blood tests: WBC 11 × 10⁹/L (eosinophils 2.5 × 10⁹/L), platelets normal, LFTs elevated (ALT 90 U/L). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Acute schistosomiasis (Katayama fever) presents 4-6 weeks after freshwater exposure with fever, cough, urticaria, and marked eosinophilia. Malaria has no eosinophilia; typhoid has relative bradycardia and rose spots; leptospirosis has conjunctival suffusion and earlier onset; amoebic liver abscess has right upper quadrant pain and no eosinophilia.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with fever, headache, and myalgia 10 days after returning from a two-week trip to rural Ghana. She took no malaria prophylaxis. On examination, temperature 39.5°C, no focal signs. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for Plasmodium falciparum
questions.explanation: Thick and thin blood films remain the gold standard for diagnosing malaria; they allow species identification and quantification of parasitaemia. RDTs may miss non-falciparum species or low parasitaemia. PCR is sensitive but not routinely available urgently. Serology indicates past infection, not acute. FBC is non-specific.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man presents with fever, severe headache, retro-orbital pain, and a macular rash three days after returning from Thailand. He has no focal signs. Temperature 39°C, platelet count 120×10⁹/L. Which diagnostic test is most appropriate in the first 5 days of illness?
questions.explanation: NS1 antigen is detectable from day 1–5 and has high sensitivity in acute dengue. IgM appears later (day 4–5 onwards), so early testing may be negative. PCR is specific but less widely available. Viral culture is slow and not routine.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man presents with a 10-day history of stepwise fever, headache, and abdominal discomfort after returning from Pakistan. He has not taken any antibiotics. Examination: temperature 38.8°C, rose spots on trunk, hepatosplenomegaly. Which is the single best test to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Widal test (Typhoid O and H agglutination)
PCR for Salmonella Typhi in blood
questions.explanation: Blood culture has the highest sensitivity (up to 90%) in the first week of typhoid fever. Stool culture is less sensitive in early illness. Widal test suffers from low specificity and cross-reactivity. PCR not routinely available. Serology has variable performance.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old student presents with fever, headache, myalgia, and abdominal pain two weeks after swimming in Lake Malawi. She has marked eosinophilia (2.5×10⁹/L) and a non-productive cough. Which diagnostic test is most appropriate for confirmation?
options: Stool microscopy for Schistosoma mansoni ova
Urine microscopy for Schistosoma haematobium ova
questions.explanation: In acute schistosomiasis (Katayama fever), eggs are not yet present in excreta; serology (detecting antibodies) is the most sensitive early test. Urine and stool microscopy may become positive after 6–8 weeks. Skin snip is for S. haematobium but not acute. Rectal biopsy more invasive and not first-line.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with fever, headache, and an eschar on his thigh 10 days after a trek in northern Thailand. He has a macular rash and lymphadenopathy. Which investigation is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
options: PCR for Orientia tsutsugamushi on eschar swab
Indirect immunofluorescence (IFA) for scrub typhus IgG
Skin biopsy of eschar for histology
questions.explanation: PCR from the eschar has high sensitivity and provides rapid confirmation of scrub typhus. IFA serology is the reference standard, but requires paired acute and convalescent samples. Weil-Felix test is non-specific and obsolete. Blood culture is rarely positive. Histology is not diagnostic for the organism.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman presents with fever, headache, and myalgia 10 days after returning from a trip to Nigeria. A blood film shows Plasmodium falciparum with a parasitaemia of 1.2%. She has no signs of severe malaria. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: UK guidelines recommend artemether-lumefantrine as first-line for uncomplicated falciparum malaria; quinine is reserved for severe or when artemether-lumefantrine is not suitable; chloroquine is ineffective due to widespread resistance; doxycycline is not used as monotherapy first-line; IV artesunate is for severe malaria.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with fever, confusion, and jaundice after returning from Ghana. His blood film shows P. falciparum with a parasitaemia of 8%. He has acute kidney injury. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Intravenous artesunate is first-line for severe malaria per UK guidelines; IV quinine is an alternative if artesunate is unavailable; oral therapy is inappropriate due to severity; exchange transfusion is not first-line; doxycycline is an adjunctive treatment, not first-line.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with fever, headache, and an eschar on her leg after trekking in Southeast Asia. Serology is positive for Orientia tsutsugamushi. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Doxycycline is first-line for scrub typhus; azithromycin is a second-line option in pregnancy or contraindications; chloramphenicol is an alternative but not first-line; ciprofloxacin is ineffective; rifampicin is not recommended as first-line.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man on long-term corticosteroids for asthma presents with abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and a rash after returning from the Caribbean. Stool microscopy reveals Strongyloides stercoralis. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Ivermectin is first-line for strongyloidiasis; albendazole is a second-line alternative; mebendazole is for pinworm; praziquantel for schistosomiasis; diethylcarbamazine for filariasis.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman in her second trimester of pregnancy presents with fever and rigors after returning from Kenya. Blood film shows P. falciparum with parasitaemia 0.5%. She has no signs of severe malaria. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, artemether-lumefantrine is recommended in the second and third trimesters; quinine plus clindamycin is an alternative but not first-line in second trimester; IV artesunate is reserved for severe malaria; chloroquine is ineffective due to resistance; doxycycline is contraindicated in pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a 3-day history of fever, headache, and generalised myalgia. He returned from Nigeria 5 days ago. He has no significant medical history and is not on any regular medication. Observations: temperature 38.9°C, heart rate 100 bpm, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 98% on air. There is no rash, no neck stiffness, and chest is clear. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give a dose of oral artemether-lumefantrine
Perform a blood film for malarial parasites
questions.explanation: In a returning traveller with fever, malaria is the most common and potentially fatal cause. The immediate priority is to confirm or exclude malaria via blood film. Starting antimalarials empirically without a diagnosis is not recommended; other infections are less likely until malaria is ruled out.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents to the GP with a 4-day history of fever, abdominal cramps, and loose watery stools. She returned from Pakistan 7 days ago. She has no vomiting, and no blood in stool. Observations are normal. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Send stool for culture and sensitivity
questions.explanation: Enteric fever (typhoid) is a key differential in a febrile traveller returning from the Indian subcontinent. Blood cultures have the highest yield early in the illness and should be taken before antibiotics. Stool cultures are less sensitive acutely. Empiric antibiotics without cultures should be avoided.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is brought to the Emergency Department with a 12-hour history of fever, confusion, and a rapidly spreading petechial rash. He returned from The Gambia 2 days ago. Observations: GCS 12, temperature 39.5°C, heart rate 120 bpm, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 24 breaths/min. He has neck stiffness. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In suspected meningococcal septicaemia/meningitis with shock and petechiae, immediate antibiotics (IV ceftriaxone) are life-saving and must not be delayed for LP or CT. Dexamethasone may be given after antibiotics, but the first priority is ceftriaxone.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with confirmed falciparum malaria (from Uganda) is on day 2 of intravenous artesunate treatment. She has been febrile but improving. Suddenly she becomes confused, agitated, and sweaty. Observations: GCS 14, temperature 37.8°C, heart rate 110 bpm, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Repeat a blood film for malaria
Give a bolus of intravenous dextrose 10%
questions.explanation: Hypoglycaemia is a common and dangerous complication of severe malaria, even during treatment. This presentation (confusion, agitation, sweating) is classic. The immediate action is to check blood glucose and treat with IV dextrose. Other options are less urgent or inappropriate.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a 5-day history of fever, severe headache, myalgia, and a maculopapular rash. He returned from Thailand 8 days ago. He has been vomiting and has mild abdominal pain. On examination, he has conjunctival injection, a positive tourniquet test, and a temperature of 39.2°C. Heart rate is 110 bpm, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Transfer to the high dependency unit
questions.explanation: This patient has dengue with warning signs (vomiting, abdominal pain, positive tourniquet test). Immediate IV isotonic crystalloids are indicated to prevent progression to shock. Blood tests are important but giving fluids is the critical first action. Paracetamol can be given but is secondary to fluid resuscitation.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with fever and headache after returning from Nigeria. Peripheral blood film shows ring forms of Plasmodium falciparum. Which single finding indicates severe malaria requiring urgent escalation?
questions.explanation: A parasite density >2% is a WHO criterion for severe malaria, indicating high risk of complications. Thrombocytopenia and mild jaundice are common but not severe malaria criteria.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man returning from Thailand has had fever for 4 days. He now has severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, and gum bleeding. Which finding most strongly indicates progression to severe dengue?
questions.explanation: A significant haematocrit rise indicates plasma leakage, the hallmark of severe dengue. A positive tourniquet test and thrombocytopenia are common but not sufficient for severe dengue classification.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman returning from Bangladesh has had a step-ladder fever for 10 days and now develops acute severe abdominal pain with board-like rigidity. Which finding most likely indicates the life-threatening complication?
options: Abdominal X-ray showing free air under the diaphragm
Blood culture positive for Salmonella typhi
questions.explanation: Free air under the diaphragm is diagnostic of intestinal perforation, a surgical emergency in typhoid. Blood culture confirms the infection but not the complication.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man returned from a freshwater swimming trip in Thailand 5 days ago. He now has fever, myalgia, jaundice, oliguria, and confusion. Which set of findings confirms severe leptospirosis with organ failure?
questions.explanation: Severe leptospirosis (Weil's disease) involves hepatic and renal dysfunction, reflected by elevated bilirubin and creatinine. Positive serology confirms infection but not severity.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man returning from South Africa has fever, headache, and an eschar with tender lymphadenopathy. Over 24 hours he becomes confused and the rash becomes petechial. Which finding indicates CNS involvement?
questions.explanation: Lymphocytic pleocytosis on CSF confirms meningoencephalitis, a severe complication of rickettsial infection. The eschar is a typical primary sign but not indicative of CNS involvement.
